id="main-content"><div class="css-iom6ik"><div class="css-dy8gfm"><span class="listed-menu css-lkkqax"><p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin no-top-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Kimi K2.5 is an open-source, multimodal model from Moonshot AI built for agentic workflows, not just chat. Rather than responding to isolated prompts, it can break down complex tasks, coordinate tool use, and produce structured outputs like tables, reports, plans, and code, across multi-step workflows.</span></p>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">What makes Kimi K2.5 especially interesting is Agent Swarm. A self-directed mode where the model can dynamically spin up and orchestrate multiple sub-agents in parallel to speed up research, verification, and execution.</span></p>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">In this tutorial, I’ll cover what Kimi K2.5 is and where it performs well, then focus on four hands-on experiments that show how Agent Swarm behaves in practice, including what it does impressively, where it falls short, and when it actually beats a single-agent setup.</span></p>
<h2 dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" id="what-is-kimi-k2.5?-<span">What Is Kimi K2.5?</h2>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Kimi K2.5 is Moonshot AI’s open-source, natively multimodal model, which is built to handle text, images, video, and documents in one system. It extends </span><a style="text-decoration: none;" href="https://www.datacamp.com/tutorial/kimi-k2" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: underline; -webkit-text-decoration-skip: none; text-decoration-skip-ink: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Kimi K2</span></a><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;"> with continued pretraining on roughly 15T mixed text and visual tokens, and it’s designed to operate not just as a chatbot, but as an agentic system that can plan, use tools, and (in swarm mode) execute tasks in parallel.</span></p>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">In practice, Kimi K2.5 stands out for three things:</span></p>
<ul style="padding-inline-start: 48px;">
<li><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: bold; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Multimodal reasoning:</span><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;"> It can read dense visuals, reason over screenshots, and follow video context to produce structured outputs.</span></li>
<li><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: bold; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Strong coding performance</span><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">: Kimi K2.5 can turn a visual reference into working code and iterate via visual debugging.</span></li>
<li><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: bold; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Agent-first execution:</span><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;"> We can run this as a single agent, a tool-augmented agent, or in Agent Swarm (beta), where it can dynamically spawn many sub-agents for wide tasks like research, extraction, comparisons, and long workflows.</span></li>
</ul>
<p dir="ltr" class="p-margin"><img style="display: block; margin-left: auto; margin-right: auto;" src="https://media.datacamp.com/cms/7dca7b4d783aa85d2a67913b441c44d5.png" alt="Kimi K2.5 performance" width="624" height="391"></p>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: center;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Source: </span><a style="text-decoration: none;" href="https://www.kimi.com/blog/kimi-k2-5.html" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: underline; -webkit-text-decoration-skip: none; text-decoration-skip-ink: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Kimi K2.5</span></a></p>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">The above plots summarize Kimi K2.5’s performance across four categories: Agents, Coding, Image, and Video, with Kimi shown in blue and other leading frontier models shown as gray bars.</span></p>
<ul style="padding-inline-start: 48px;">
<li><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: bold; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Agents:</span><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;"> Kimi is particularly strong on agent-style benchmarks, leading on HLE-Full (50.2) and BrowseComp (74.9), and performing competitively on DeepSearchQA (77.1). This supports the agentic positioning.</span></li>
<li><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: bold; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Coding:</span><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;"> It is competitive on SWE-Bench Verified and Multilingual benchmarks, with top closed models and clearly strong among open options, especially for practical engineering tasks.</span></li>
<li><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: bold; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Image:</span><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;"> Kimi also performs well on multimodal reasoning and document-heavy understanding, with solid MMMU/MathVision scores.</span></li>
<li><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: bold; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Video:</span><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;"> This model shows strong performance on LongVideoBench bechmark, which is a good signal for long-horizon video understanding rather than single-frame VQA.</span></li>
</ul>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">You can try Kimi K2.5 in multiple ways:</span></p>
<ul style="padding-inline-start: 48px;">
<li><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Web-based </span><a style="text-decoration: none;" href="https://www.kimi.com/" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: underline; -webkit-text-decoration-skip: none; text-decoration-skip-ink: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">chat</span></a></li>
<li><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">API: <code><a style="text-decoration: none;" href="https://platform.moonshot.ai" target="_blank" rel="noopener">https://platform.moonshot.ai</a></code></span></li>
<li><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Open-source weights: </span><a style="text-decoration: none;" href="https://huggingface.co/moonshotai/Kimi-K2.5/tree/main" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: underline; -webkit-text-decoration-skip: none; text-decoration-skip-ink: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Hugging Face</span></a></li>
<li><a style="text-decoration: none;" href="https://www.kimi.com/code/en?track_id=5928d89a-6b60-453c-a4a6-161a0da3d1c8" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: underline; -webkit-text-decoration-skip: none; text-decoration-skip-ink: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Kimi Code</span></a><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">: Via terminal and IDE integration for agentic coding</span></li>
</ul>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">For reproducible benchmarks, Moonshot recommends using the official API or verified providers via Kimi Vendor Verifier.</span></p>
<h2 dir="ltr" id="what-is-agent-swarm-in-kimi-k2.5?-<span">What is Agent Swarm in Kimi K2.5?</h2>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Most multi-agent setups today are still hand-built; you define roles, wire a workflow, and hope the orchestration holds up as tasks get bigger. Kimi K2.5 Agent Swarm flips that model. </span></p>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Instead of predefining agents and pipelines, K2.5 can self-direct a swarm, deciding when to parallelize, how many agents to spawn, what tools to use, and how to merge results, based on the task itself.</span></p>
<h2 dir="ltr" id=""><img loading="lazy" style="display: block; margin-left: auto; margin-right: auto;" src="https://media.datacamp.com/cms/93e5b22cab37f65db340fa48d3892b4c.png" alt="Kimi Chat" width="624" height="471"></h2>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">It can autonomously create and coordinate an agent swarm of up to 100 sub-agents, executing parallel workflows across up to 1,500 tool calls, with no predefined roles.</span></p>
<h3 dir="ltr" id="how-does-kimi-k2.5-agent-swarm-work?-<span">How does Kimi K2.5 Agent Swarm work?</h3>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Under the hood, Agent Swarm introduces a trainable orchestrator that learns to decompose work into parallel subtasks and schedule them efficiently:</span></p>
<ul style="padding-inline-start: 48px;">
<li><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Orchestrator decomposes the task into parallelizable chunks, such as finding sources, extracting data, verifying claims, and formatting output.</span></li>
<li><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">It then instantiates sub-agents, which are typically frozen workers spun up to execute specific subtasks on demand.</span></li>
<li><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Sub-agents run concurrently, each using tools such as search, browsing, code interpreter, and file creation independently.</span></li>
<li><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">It finally aggregates and reconciles outputs into a final structured deliverable like a report, spreadsheet, doc, or codebase plan.</span></li>
</ul>
<h3 dir="ltr" id="why-parl(parallel-agent-reinforcement-learning)-matters-<span">Why PARL(Parallel-Agent Reinforcement Learning) matters</h3>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Kimi K2.5’s swarm behavior is trained using Parallel-Agent Reinforcement Learning (</span><a style="text-decoration: none;" href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2106.07551#:~:text=Despite%20remarkable%20prior%20arts%20of,available%20at%20this%20https%20URL." target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: underline; -webkit-text-decoration-skip: none; text-decoration-skip-ink: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">PARL</span></a><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">), a training setup that makes parallelism itself a learnable skill. This matters because naive multi-agent systems often fail in two ways:</span></p>
<ul style="padding-inline-start: 48px;">
<li><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: bold; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Serial collapse:</span><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;"> Even with many agents available, the system defaults to a slow single-threaded pattern.</span></li>
<li><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: bold; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Fake parallelism:</span><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;"> It spawns agents, but the work isn’t actually parallel or doesn’t reduce latency.</span></li>
</ul>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">PARL addresses this by shaping rewards over training. It encourages parallelism early and gradually shifts optimization toward end-to-end task quality, preventing fake parallelism. To make the optimization latency-aware, K2.5 evaluates performance using Critical Steps because spawning more agents only helps if it actually shortens the slowest path of execution rather than inflating coordination overhead.</span></p>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">When the task is wide and tool-heavy, Agent Swarm can materially reduce time-to-output. Moonshot reports that, compared to a single-agent setup, K2.5 Swarm can cut execution time by ~3x to 4.5x, and internal evaluations show up to ~80% reduction in end-to-end runtime on complex workloads through true parallelization. </span></p>
<h2 dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" id="kimi-k2.5-examples-and-observations-<span">Kimi K2.5 Examples and Observations</h2>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">In this section, I’ll share my firsthand experience testing Kimi K2.5 Agent Swarm across diverse scenarios. Each example highlights how the swarm decomposes tasks, allocates agents, and where this approach genuinely helps or falls short in practice.</span></p>
<h3 dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" id="research-swarm-<span">Research swarm</h3>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">For my first experiment, I tested whether Kimi K2.5 Agent Swarm could tackle a high-stakes, real-world task like drafting a deployment plan for open-source LLMs.</span></p>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: bold; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Prompt:</span><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;"> </span></p>
<pre class="language-markdown code-block" tabindex="0"><div class="parent"><code class="language-markdown">I want you to research best practices for deploying open-source LLMs in production. Focus on real deployment patterns rather than theory. If possible, have different agents look into, Inference stacks like vLLM, TGI, llama.cpp, Quantization strategies and hardware trade-offs, and cost control techniques. I want a short, structured guide with common architectures, trade-offs, and concrete recommendations for small teams vs large-scale deployments.</code><div style="display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 8px; position: absolute; top: 16px; right: 16px; z-index: 1;"><button class="code-copy-button" data-trackid="copy-code"><svg aria-hidden="true" height="16" width="16" viewBox="0 0 18 18" <p dir="ltr" class="p-margin"><img loading="lazy" style="display: block; margin-left: auto; margin-right: auto;" src="https://media.datacamp.com/cms/7da17084a896ec7ea47ac7cfd086f438.png" alt="Research swarm" width="624" height="359"></p>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">In this demo, Kimi K2.5 Agent Swarm immediately decomposed the prompt into parallel research tracks and spun up three dedicated sub-agents, namely <code>InferenceStackResearcher</code>, <code>QuantizationHardwareResearcher</code>, and <code>CostControlResearcher</code>. While each subagent name refers to the work they were allocated, it then fanned out work across multiple worker personas. </span></p>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin no-bottom-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">What I liked most was how the final output was a clean comparison table for inference stacks, along with concise recommendations and notes for quantization. You can also use the task progress bar to see how it decomposed the work upfront and tracked each subtask to completion.</span></p></span></div></div><div class="css-9vmp85"><div class="css-j7kvgs"><iframe src="https://fast.wistia.net/embed/iframe/bz0arnicrv" width="560" height="315" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen="" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture" loading="lazy" title="Embedded blog video" class="css-oapqm9"></iframe></div></div><div class="css-mritbm"><div class="css-dy8gfm"><span class="listed-menu css-lkkqax"><p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin no-top-margin no-bottom-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">However, the downside is that some claims in the final write-up feel under-cited or too absolute, and the guide would be stronger with links, sources, and clearer assumptions (hardware, model size, batch size, context length), since production advice is highly context-dependent. Overall, it handled a complex, multi-part prompt and returned a crisp, immediately usable playbook that looks production-ready. </span></p></span></div></div><div class="css-9vmp85"><div class="css-j7kvgs"><iframe src="https://fast.wistia.net/embed/iframe/avieecij7f" width="560" height="315" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen="" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture" loading="lazy" title="Embedded blog video" class="css-oapqm9"></iframe></div></div><div class="css-mritbm"><div class="css-dy8gfm"><span class="listed-menu css-lkkqax"><h3 dir="ltr" id="dataset-creation-and-annotation-<span" class="no-top-margin">Dataset creation and annotation</h3>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Next, I tested Agent Swarm on a dataset-building task by asking Kimi K2.5 to generate a 100-item math word-problem benchmark with solutions and difficulty labels. I also explicitly requested it use </span><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: bold; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">20 agents</span><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;"> to see how closely it follows the swarm-size constraint while maintaining quality.</span></p>
<h3 dir="ltr" id=""><img style="display: block; margin-left: auto; margin-right: auto;" src="https://media.datacamp.com/cms/24999089c365e82a5b45e36a98217f3c.png" alt="Dataset creation and annotation" width="624" height="355"></h3>
<p dir="ltr" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: bold; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Prompt:</span><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;"> </span></p>
<pre class="language-markdown code-block" tabindex="0"><div class="parent"><code class="language-markdown">Build a small benchmark dataset of 100 math word problems with step-by-step solutions and difficulty labels. Use at least 20 agents to generate problems in parallel, verify solutions independently, and calibrate difficulty.</code><div style="display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 8px; position: absolute; top: 16px; right: 16px; z-index: 1;"><button class="code-copy-button" data-trackid="copy-code"><svg aria-hidden="true" height="16" width="16" viewBox="0 0 18 18" class="css-6su6fj"><path 15.068S27.46 34.136 19.138 34.136V4ZM16.398 20.133c-7.733 0-14.002 6.27-14.002 14.003h14.002V20.133Z" fill="var(--wf-brand--text, #05192D)"></path></svg></a></div></div></pre>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Kimi K2.5 Agent Swarm treated dataset creation as a three-phase pipeline, including generation, verification, and calibration. Although the prompt asked for at least 20 agents, the system autonomously spun up 25 sub-agents, prioritizing correctness over strict adherence to the requested count. It was interesting to observe that about five agents worked actively at a time while others queued and resumed as earlier subtasks completed, which suggests an internal scheduling mechanism. </span></p>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin no-bottom-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Each generation agent handled a distinct math domain and produced five problems with step-by-step solutions, after which 10 agents were allocated to verification, and a smaller set of five agents focused on difficulty calibration. </span></p></span></div></div><div class="css-9vmp85"><div class="css-j7kvgs"><iframe src="https://fast.wistia.net/embed/iframe/9cii5ng6uq" width="560" height="315" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen="" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture" loading="lazy" title="Embedded blog video" class="css-oapqm9"></iframe></div></div><div class="css-mritbm"><div class="css-dy8gfm"><span class="listed-menu css-lkkqax"><p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin no-top-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">The standout strength here is the clear separation of concerns and redundancy in verification, which materially improves dataset quality. The main downside is latency and a sequential bottleneck. </span></p>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">The full run took roughly 20–25 minutes, making this approach better suited for high-quality benchmark creation than rapid iteration. Even with 25 agents, some agents had to wait for others to finish, meaning the swarm is still limited by the logic of the steps.</span></p>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin no-bottom-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Still, the ability to dynamically adjust agent count for each subtask, reuse idle agents, and converge on a labeled dataset is a strong demonstration of how Agent Swarm behaves. </span></p></span></div></div><div class="css-9vmp85"><div class="css-j7kvgs"><iframe src="https://fast.wistia.net/embed/iframe/0128fuxlsq" width="560" height="315" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen="" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture" loading="lazy" title="Embedded blog video" class="css-oapqm9"></iframe></div></div><div class="css-mritbm"><div class="css-dy8gfm"><span class="listed-menu css-lkkqax"><h3 dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" id="multimodal-qa-at-scale -<span" class="no-top-margin">Multimodal QA at scale </h3>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Next, I pushed Kimi K2.5 into a multimodal workflow by providing multiple nutrition-label images and asking for a structured comparison. The goal was to see how well it can extract, normalize, and verify visual data.</span></p>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: bold; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Prompt</span><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">: </span></p>
<pre class="language-markdown code-block" tabindex="0"><div class="parent"><code class="language-markdown">I’m sharing images of nutrition labels from 7 packaged food items. Analyze them and produce a clear comparison specifically:
Extract fields like: calories, protein, total fat, sugar, sodium,  from each label.Normalize all values to a per-100g basis so items are comparableDouble-check numeric consistency (e.g. serving size vs totals)Rank the products from healthiest to least healthy, explaining the reasoningReturn a comparison table with normalized values for all items. Also include a summary highlighting, best high-protein option, the lowest sugar option, and items to avoid and why.
Assume the labels may vary in format and serving size. Resolve ambiguities where possible and note any missing or unclear values.</code><div style="display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 8px; position: absolute; top: 16px; right: 16px; z-index: 1;"><button class="code-copy-button" data-trackid="copy-code"><svg aria-hidden="true" height="16" width="16" viewBox="0 0 18 18" class="css-6su6fj"><path fill="currentColor" d="M7 4H5a4 4 0 0 1 15.068S27.46 34.136 19.138 34.136V4ZM16.398 20.133c-7.733 0-14.002 6.27-14.002 14.003h14.002V20.133Z" fill="var(--wf-brand--text, #05192D)"></path></svg></a></div></div></pre>
<p dir="ltr" class="p-margin"><img style="display: block; margin-left: auto; margin-right: auto;" src="https://media.datacamp.com/cms/af71b940915fff930a16fadcc905fd1b.png" alt="Multimodal QA at scale" width="624" height="357"></p>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">In this multimodal QA experiment, Kimi K2.5 Agent Swarm decomposed the task around one label per sub-agent, spinning up seven parallel sub-agents to extract nutritional fields from each image while a coordinating agent handled normalization, cross-checking, and ranking. </span></p>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">An interesting thing about Kimi is that the constraints you specify in the prompt (extraction, normalization, verification, ranking) effectively become the blueprint for how Kimi instantiates and assigns sub-agents.</span></p>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin no-bottom-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">What stood out for me is how the swarm treated verification as an important step, i.e, after extraction, the orchestrator explicitly reconciled serving sizes, normalized values, and flagged ambiguities instead of silently guessing. The final synthesis step, which ranked products and called out best high-protein and low-sugar options, demonstrated strong aggregation logic across visual inputs.</span></p></span></div></div><div class="css-9vmp85"><div class="css-j7kvgs"><iframe src="https://fast.wistia.net/embed/iframe/ktdmpyiltj" width="560" height="315" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen="" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture" loading="lazy" title="Embedded blog video" class="css-oapqm9"></iframe></div></div><div class="css-mritbm"><div class="css-dy8gfm"><span class="listed-menu css-lkkqax"><p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin no-top-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">On the downside, analyzing 7 high-res images with a swarm is compute-heavy, making it a premium feature rather than a quick-chat tool. </span></p>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">This example also exposed a current limitation; while image-based multimodal analysis worked well, direct video input was not supported, so a follow-up attempt to generate a recipe from a cooking video could not be executed. </span></p>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin no-bottom-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Overall, this demo highlights Agent Swarm’s strength in parallel visual extraction and structured reconciliation.</span></p></span></div></div><div class="css-9vmp85"><div class="css-j7kvgs"><iframe src="https://fast.wistia.net/embed/iframe/3rt1ra2wbt" width="560" height="315" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen="" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture" loading="lazy" title="Embedded blog video" class="css-oapqm9"></iframe></div></div><div class="css-mritbm"><div class="css-dy8gfm"><span class="listed-menu css-lkkqax"><h3 dir="ltr" id="coding-up-a-game-<span" class="no-top-margin">Coding up a game</h3>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Finally, I tested Kimi K2.5’s coding capabilities by asking it to build a fully interactive Bubble Shooter game from scratch. This experiment highlights how the model handles end-to-end, stateful code generation where coherence matters more than parallelism.</span></p>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: bold; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Prompt: </span></p>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">```markdown</span></p>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Build a fully playable Bubble Shooter game that runs locally in a web browser. The game should support mouse and touch aiming, show a live aim guideline, fire colored bubbles from the bottom center, bounce shots off side walls, and snap bubbles to a grid at the top on collision. </span></p>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Implement core gameplay logic including popping groups of three or more connected bubbles of the same color, dropping any disconnected clusters, tracking and displaying score, showing a next-bubble preview, and ending the game when bubbles cross a danger line near the bottom.</span></p>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Keep visuals simple but responsive, include a restart button, and finish with brief instructions on how to run and test the game locally.</span></p>
<p dir="ltr" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">```</span><img style="display: block; margin-left: auto; margin-right: auto;" src="https://media.datacamp.com/cms/7f40985034f151426cf35bd1d9dea301.png" alt="Coding up a game" width="624" height="340"></p>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">In this game-building experiment, Kimi K2.5 Agent Swarm made a notably conservative choice by allocating just a single sub-agent to handle the entire task. </span></p>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">That decision itself is an interesting observation because the system correctly recognized that this was a tightly coupled, stateful coding problem where heavy parallelism would add coordination overhead without any benefit. </span></p>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin no-bottom-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">The agent produced a complete, locally runnable Bubble Shooter implementation with aiming, wall bounces, grid snapping, match-three popping, score tracking, and restart logic, showing both gameplay mechanics and UI flow. </span></p></span></div></div><div class="css-9vmp85"><div class="css-j7kvgs"><iframe src="https://fast.wistia.net/embed/iframe/pjkz1y79ra" width="560" height="315" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen="" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture" loading="lazy" title="Embedded blog video" class="css-oapqm9"></iframe></div></div><div class="css-mritbm"><div class="css-dy8gfm"><span class="listed-menu css-lkkqax"><p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin no-top-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">What I particularly liked is that the output felt coherent and integrated, but the trade-off, however, is that refinement and polish would likely require iterative prompting, since everything runs through a single agent. </span></p>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin no-bottom-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Overall, this example nicely demonstrates that Agent Swarm in Kimi K2.5 is not about always spawning more agents, but about using parallelism only when it genuinely improves outcomes.</span></p></span></div></div><div class="css-9vmp85"><div class="css-j7kvgs"><iframe src="https://fast.wistia.net/embed/iframe/8h71csntll" width="560" height="315" frameborder="0" allowfullscreen="" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture" loading="lazy" title="Embedded blog video" class="css-oapqm9"></iframe></div></div><div class="css-mritbm"><div class="css-dy8gfm"><span class="listed-menu css-lkkqax"><h2 dir="ltr" id="conclusion-<span" class="no-top-margin">Conclusion</h2>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">In this tutorial, Kimi K2.5 demonstrated an ability to decompose complex tasks, allocate agents dynamically, and produce structured, decision-ready outputs that would traditionally require a small team rather than a single model.</span></p>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">At the same time, the examples make its boundaries clear. </span></p>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Agent Swarm excels on wide, tool-heavy workflows like research, dataset construction, and multimodal extraction, but offers little advantage for tightly coupled, stateful tasks such as interactive game development. </span></p>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">It also introduces real trade-offs around latency, cost, and iteration speed, especially when verification and reconciliation are prioritized over raw throughput.</span></p>
<p dir="ltr" style="text-align: justify;" class="p-margin"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">Overall, Kimi K2.5 offers a compelling blueprint for where agentic systems are headed. For developers and researchers comfortable experimenting with agent-based workflows, it’s one of the most interesting models to explore today.</span></p>
<p class="p-margin no-bottom-margin"><strong id="docs-internal-guid-3ce3318b-7fff-bc7b-faf9-e7b178e2f068" style="font-weight: normal;"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">If you’re keen to learn more about how agentic AI works, I recommend checking out the </span><a style="text-decoration: none;" href="https://www.datacamp.com/tracks/ai-agent-fundamentals" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span style="background-color: transparent; font-weight: 400; font-style: normal; font-variant: normal; text-decoration: underline; -webkit-text-decoration-skip: none; text-decoration-skip-ink: none; vertical-align: baseline; white-space: pre-wrap;">AI Agent Fundamentals skill track</span></a>.</strong></p></span></div></div><div class="css-lwcnm9"><div class="css-inceod"><span class="listed-menu css-1yz7e9k"><h2 class="css-ov6cx no-top-margin no-bottom-margin" id="faq">Kimi K2.5 and Agent Swarm FAQs</h2></span><div class="css-1yz7e9k"><div class="faq-entry css-1lgzsq6"><h3 style="align-items:center;display:flex;font-weight:800;justify-content:space-between;line-height:125%" class="css-a77qhl">Is Kimi K2.5 truly open source and free to use?<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="13" height="8" class="css-18x2vi3"><path fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-linejoin="round" stroke-width="2" d="M12.1.92 6.55 6.47 1 .92"></path></svg></h3><div class="css-b9gp7g"><div class="css-nstkxm"><div class="css-khf1ag"><span class="listed-menu css-lkkqax"><p class="no-top-margin no-bottom-margin">Yes, Moonshot AI has released Kimi K2.5 under a <strong data-path-to-node="2" data-index-in-node="99">Modified MIT License</strong> with weights available on Hugging Face. However, because it is a massive <strong data-path-to-node="2" data-index-in-node="193">1.04 Trillion parameter</strong> model (Mixture-of-Experts), running it locally requires enterprise-grade hardware (e.g., multiple H100 GPUs). For most users, the official API or Web UI is the most practical access point.</p></span></div></div></div></div><div class="faq-entry css-1lgzsq6"><h3 style="align-items:center;display:flex;font-weight:800;justify-content:space-between;line-height:125%" class="css-a77qhl">What is the Kimi K2.5 API pricing?<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="13" height="8" class="css-167dpqb"><path fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-linejoin="round" stroke-width="2" d="M12.1.92 6.55 6.47 1 .92"></path></svg></h3><div class="css-1du38k8"><div class="css-nstkxm"><div class="css-khf1ag"><span class="listed-menu css-lkkqax"><p class="no-top-margin no-bottom-margin">Kimi K2.5 offers competitive API pricing: <strong data-path-to-node="3" data-index-in-node="87">$0.60 per 1M input tokens</strong> (dropping to <strong data-path-to-node="3" data-index-in-node="126">$0.10</strong> for cached inputs) and <strong data-path-to-node="3" data-index-in-node="155">$3.00 per 1M output tokens</strong>. </p></span></div></div></div></div><div class="faq-entry css-1lgzsq6"><h3 style="align-items:center;display:flex;font-weight:800;justify-content:space-between;line-height:125%" class="css-a77qhl">What is the difference between Standard Chat and Agent Swarm?<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="13" height="8" class="css-167dpqb"><path fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-linejoin="round" stroke-width="2" d="M12.1.92 6.55 6.47 1 .92"></path></svg></h3><div class="css-1du38k8"><div class="css-nstkxm"><div class="css-khf1ag"><span class="listed-menu css-lkkqax"><p class="no-top-margin no-bottom-margin">Standard chat is linear and single-threaded. <strong data-path-to-node="4" data-index-in-node="110">Agent Swarm</strong> is a parallel orchestrator: it can autonomously break a prompt into sub-tasks and spawn up to <strong data-path-to-node="4" data-index-in-node="216">100 specialized sub-agents</strong> to execute them simultaneously. Swarm is best for "wide" tasks like deep research or bulk data extraction, while Chat is better for stateful, sequential tasks like coding a game.</p></span></div></div></div></div><div class="faq-entry css-1lgzsq6"><h3 style="align-items:center;display:flex;font-weight:800;justify-content:space-between;line-height:125%" class="css-a77qhl">Does Kimi K2.5 support video input?<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="13" height="8" class="css-167dpqb"><path fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-linejoin="round" stroke-width="2" d="M12.1.92 6.55 6.47 1 .92"></path></svg></h3><div class="css-1du38k8"><div class="css-nstkxm"><div class="css-khf1ag"><span class="listed-menu css-lkkqax"><p class="no-top-margin no-bottom-margin">Yes, the model is natively multimodal and supports video input up to <strong data-path-to-node="5" data-index-in-node="108">2K resolution</strong>. While it excels at long-context video benchmarks (like LongVideoBench), the feature is currently <strong data-path-to-node="5" data-index-in-node="220">experimental</strong>, meaning it may occasionally struggle with complex instruction following in video-based agent workflows compared to static images.</p></span></div></div></div></div><div class="faq-entry css-1lgzsq6"><h3 style="align-items:center;display:flex;font-weight:800;justify-content:space-between;line-height:125%" class="css-a77qhl">What is the context window size for Kimi K2.5?<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="13" height="8" class="css-167dpqb"><path fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-linejoin="round" stroke-width="2" d="M12.1.92 6.55 6.47 1 .92"></path></svg></h3><div class="css-1du38k8"><div class="css-nstkxm"><div class="css-khf1ag"><span class="listed-menu css-lkkqax"><p class="no-top-margin no-bottom-margin">Kimi K2.5 supports a <strong data-path-to-node="6" data-index-in-node="71">256,000 token context window</strong> (approx. 200,000 words). This ultra-long context allows the model to process entire books, massive codebases, or long legal documents in a single pass without needing to chunk data, which is critical for maintaining accuracy in large swarms.</p></span></div></div></div></div></div></div></div><div class="author-biography css-9uwffg"><hr class="css-h24qx8"><div class="css-z5fbhm"><div class="hide-in-percy css-1wxyybu"><div From automating workflows to helping users navigate complex tasks, agents can search, reason, and act on your behalf. In this course, you’ll learn how to build agents using smolagents, a lightweight Python framework developed by Hugging Face.\n\nGet Hands-On With Code Agents and Tools\n\nYou’ll start by understanding what makes code agents different and why they're so powerful. Then, you’ll build your first agent from scratch, using smolagents to generate and execute Python code. You’ll also learn how to plug in built-in tools and create custom tools to extend what your agents can do.\n\nMake Agents Smarter With RAG and Memory\n\nNext, you’ll use retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to help agents pull info from large document collections. You’ll take things further by building agentic RAG systems—agents that reason over multiple steps to get better answers. You’ll also learn how to add memory so agents can handle follow-up questions naturally and keep track of what’s already been done.\n\nCoordinate Multi-Agent Systems and Validate Outputs\n\nIn the final chapter, you’ll build multi-agent systems that coordinate specialist agents through a manager. In this course, you\u0026rsquo;ll learn how to build agents using smolagents, a lightweight Python framework developed by Hugging Face. Get Hands-On With Code Agents and Tools You\u0026rsquo;ll start by understanding what makes code agents different and why theyre so powerful. Then, you\u0026rsquo;ll build your first agent from scratch, using smolagents to generate and execute Python code. You\u0026rsquo;ll also learn how to plug in built-in tools and create custom tools to extend what your agents can do. Make Agents Smarter With RAG and Memory Next, you\u0026rsquo;ll use retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to help agents pull info from large document collections. You\u0026rsquo;ll take things further by building agentic RAG systems\u0026mdash;agents that reason over multiple steps to get better answers. You\u0026rsquo;ll also learn how to add memory so agents can handle follow-up questions naturally and keep track of what\u0026rsquo;s already been done. Coordinate Multi-Agent Systems and Validate Outputs In the final chapter, you\u0026rsquo;ll build multi-agent systems that coordinate specialist agents through a manager. You\u0026rsquo;ll add planning intervals, use callbacks for insight into agent behavior, and validate final answers, so your agents stay reliable and user-friendly. By the end of the course, you\u0026rsquo;ll know how to build agents that think ahead, work together, and get things done.\u003c/p\u003e","algoliaItem":"43797__AI Agents with Hugging Face smolagents__app.datacamp.com/learn/courses/ai-agents-with-hugging-face-smolagents__course"},{"id":36539,"description":"\u003cp\u003eBuild a customer-support assistant step-by-step with Google\u0026rsquo;s Agent Development Kit: start from a one-line greeting bot, then add tools for FAQ look-ups, policy guardrails for safety, and multi-agent delegation for complex queries. Throughout the course, you\u0026rsquo;ll wire up Gemini, call external APIs, and orchestrate specialist sub-agents, focusing on hands-on agent design.
